J.R.D.Tata

# J.R.D.Tata

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jul 29, 2022

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8Y5L!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1e17655b-99b7-4512-be62-97a69ac60ec7_602x658.jpeg)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8Y5L!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1e17655b-99b7-4512-be62-97a69ac60ec7_602x658.jpeg "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If Jamsedji Tata laid the foundation, it was J.R.D.Tata who built on that foundation, expanded Tata Group into a giant.

Jehangir Ratanji Dadabhoy Tata was born on 29 July 1904 to an Indian Parsi family in Paris, France. He was the second child of businessman Ratanji Dadabhoy Tata and his French wife, Suzanne "Sooni" Brière.

His father was the first cousin of Jamsetji Tata, a pioneer industrialist in India. He had one elder sister Sylla, a younger sister Rodabeh and two younger brothers Darab and Jamshed (called Jimmy) Tata. His sister, Sylla, was married to Dinshaw Maneckji Petit, the third baronet of Petits.

A student of the Cathedral and John Connon School, he moved to Yokohoma in 1917 along with his family, and lived there for two years attending an American school. He moved to London, after his father joined Tata Group, and it was during this time his mother passed away too.

After the loss of his mother, his father’s decision to send him back to England wasn't just about academics; it was about resilience, rebuilding, and preparing him to carry forward a legacy with his own stamp of brilliance.

At Cambridge, his curiosity leaned toward Engineering—an instinctive choice for someone mesmerized by flight, structure, and innovation. Even though circumstances didn’t allow him to finish his degree, this phase sharpened his intellect and deepened his appreciation for discipline and precision.

However he had to enlist in the French Army, for at least a year, and was posted into a regiment of spahis. After discovering his fluency in French and English, he was assigned the job of a secretary in the office. Though he planned to return to Cambridge and continue his studies, his father bought him back to India.

Renouncing his French citizenship in 1919, JRD became an Indian citizen, married Thelma Vicaji, the niece of Jack Vicaji, a colourful lawyer whom he had hired earlier to defend him on a charge of driving his Bugatti too fast along Bombay's main promenade, Marine Drive. Earlier he had been engaged to Dinbai Mehta, the future mother of The Economist editor Shapur Kharegat.

Though born a Parsi, JRD was personally an agnostic. His agnosticism wasn’t a rejection—it felt more like a quiet assertion of individual philosophy. Guided by the essence of Zoroastrianism—*Humata, Hukhta, Huvarshta*—he lived the values that transcend dogma: integrity, compassion, and action.

The fact that he respected the core tenets but distanced himself from rituals and exclusivity shows a mind shaped by reason and openness. That kind of clarity likely influenced how he ran the Tata empire—with an eye for merit, humanity, and the future, rather than boundaries or orthodoxy.

Inspired by Louis Bleriot, he took to flying and became the first Indian to obtain a pilot’s license in 1929, and founded India’s first commercial airline Tata Airlines along with his friend Neville Vincent in 1932, that later became Air India in 1946. He also flew the first commercial mail flight to Juhu, in a de Havilland Puss Moth in 1932 itself.

History Under Your Feet is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Subscribe

He can rightly be called the father of Indian aviation, piloted the very first commercial air transport service from Drigh in Karachi to Madras in 1932. His biggest achievement though wud be launching Air India in 1946, India's first international airline, a baby he nurtured so lovingly, and built up to a top notch airline. When Air India, was nationalized in 1953, he fought against the decision tooth and nail. Sadly he could not prevent it.

However JRD still remained it's Chairman and later was bestowed title of Hon. Air Commodore.However he was forced out in 1977 by Morarji Desai, and though Indira asked him to take charge later, he refused. It is ironical and sad that not one airport in India is named after JRD, the father of Indian aviation.

He joined Tata Sons as an unpaid apprentice in 1925, that became the prologue to one of India’s most transformative industrial journeys. When J.R.D. Tata stepped into leadership in 1938—just 34 and full of unorthodox vision—he didn’t just inherit a title. He reshaped what industry could look like when steered by conscience.

In an era when shortcuts were commonplace, he held fast to ethical conduct—refusing bribery and black-market dealings, even if it meant a slower climb. Steel, chemicals, hospitality, engineering—he orchestrated growth like a maestro, making Tata Group not just a business giant but a benchmark of trust. His succession from Nowroji Saklatwala carried forward family legacy, but also sowed a cultural shift—introducing values of accountability and purpose.

J.R.D. Tata didn’t merely scale numbers; he nurtured a legacy. From \$100 million to over \$5 billion in assets, and from 14 enterprises to a sprawling ecosystem of 95—this wasn’t expansion for its own sake. It was purposeful, principled evolution rooted in nation-building.

The group covered every cornerstone of Indian industry, **steel** with Tata Steel’s global rise, **energy** through Tata Power’s sustainable push,**hospitality** via Taj Hotels’ elegance and heritage , **Technology & Research** laying groundwork that led to the likes of TCS and Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, and automotive sector through Tata Motors that was launched in 1945.

As trustee of Sir Dorabji Tata Trust from its inception in 1932, he rendered around 50 years of service, turning it into a catalyst for transformation. **Tata Memorial Centre in 1941***,* Asia’s first cancer research and treatment facility—life-saving care born of foresight. TISS in 1936, a pioneering space for social sciences that continues to shape policy and thought leadership. TIFR in 1945 that served as India’s nucleus for fundamental research in physics, mathematics, and computer science, and NCPA( National Center for Performing Arts), a sanctuary for creative expression.

J.R.D. Tata understood that industry wasn’t built by machines alone—but by hearts, hands, and dignity. His belief that *“only a satisfied worker can produce results”* wasn’t just a slogan—it was a blueprint for ethical enterprise.

He initiated a policy of **employee Association with Management**, a bold step to democratize the workplace, giving workers an audible voice in corporate decisions. At a time when long shifts were normalized, the 8 hr workday was a declaration of respect for personal time and labor equity. The policy of free medical aid to workers, symbolized health as a right, and Provident Fund, Accident compensation, embedded security into employment, treating workers not just as resources, but as individuals with futures and families.

JRD was good friends with Nehru, hut had major disagreements with him over his socialist policies and population control, that weren't mere ideological riffs; they were deep philosophical divergences on how India should rise. J.R.D. championed free enterprise and ethical capitalism.

He believed efficiency, innovation, and opportunity—not state control—should power India's growth. Where Nehru saw strength in numbers, J.R.D. saw a looming challenge. He advocated for population control as a necessity for sustainable development.

And not content with just passive charity, JRD sold his personal shares and even his Mumbai apartment to fund the **JRD Tata Trust**. He believed mere charity would not uplift lives, only meaningful, dignified opportunities will. This Trust helped build livelihoods with long-term vision, not short-term applause.

For him, *national interest* wasn’t defined by borders or slogans—it was about building enduring capabilities in science, education, and well-being. While others saw numbers as strength, J.R.D. saw beyond the surface—realizing that unchecked growth strained resources, healthcare, education, and opportunities.

He helped establish this Institution of Population Studies to empower India with research-driven strategies on demographic trends, family planning, and sustainable development. The UN Award in 1992, underlined his proactive role in addressing population challenges with foresight and compassion—long before it became a mainstream discourse.

J.R.D. Tata wasn’t simply a corporate trailblazer—he was a societal architect, threading empathy and foresight into India’s industrial evolution. As a founding member of NCAER, India's first independent economic policy think tank in 1956, he understood that sound research was the bedrock of effective governance and economic planning.

Transforming Tata Computer Centre into Tata Consultancy Services in 1968 laid the foundation for India’s rise in global IT services—before the world knew how big it would become. His eye for innovation and lifestyle industries led to Titan, a brand that elegantly fused technology, design, and Indian identity.

In 1979 Tata Steel introduced a “home to home” policy, that deemed a worker being "at work" from the moment he leaves home for work until he returns home from work. This made the company financially liable to the worker for any mishap on the way to and from work. Jamshedpur was declared as UN Global Compact City, a living testament to his belief, that industrial progress must uplift daily life be it roads, sanitation or welfare.

To look at JRD Tata as a mere businessman would not be doing justice to the great man, he was a multifacted personality, a visionary who saw ahead, a man who invested his money in many a noble cause. One book I would reccomend on JRD is R.M.Lala's biography "Beyond the Last Blue Mountain" to know him more.He was a thinker among businessmen, a visionary who saw the future, a man who always thought for the welfare of India.
